tips.title: How to Apply for BMR Housing
tips.subtitle: A step-by-step guide to navigating the application process across Bay Area cities.
tips.step1.title: 1. Check Your Income Eligibility
tips.step1.p1: BMR units are restricted by Area Median Income (AMI) levels. Each listing specifies which AMI bracket it serves — typically 50%, 80%, or 120% AMI. Use our AMI Calculator to see where your household falls.
tips.step1.p2: Your total household income includes all adults who will live in the unit. This means wages, self-employment income, Social Security, and most other regular income sources.
tips.step2.title: 2. Gather Your Documents
tips.step2.p1: Most applications require similar documentation. Having these ready before you apply saves time and avoids missed deadlines:
tips.step2.common: Commonly required: Government-issued photo ID for all adults, tax returns (2 most recent years), pay stubs (2–3 most recent months), bank statements (2–3 most recent months), proof of current address, employer verification letter.
tips.step2.sometimes: Sometimes required: Credit report, rental history/landlord references, asset statements (investments, retirement accounts).
tips.step3.title: 3. Apply to Multiple Listings
tips.step3.p1: BMR housing uses a lottery system in most Bay Area cities. Being selected is partially luck — so apply to every listing you qualify for. There is no penalty for applying to multiple properties, and most applications are free.
tips.step3.p2: Each property has its own application. Some cities use online portals (like San Francisco's DAHLIA), while others accept paper applications or email submissions. We link directly to each application from every listing on our site.
tips.step4.title: 4. Watch Your Deadlines
tips.step4.p1: Application windows are typically 30–60 days. Late applications are almost never accepted. We display deadline status on every listing — green means open, yellow means closing soon, gray means closed.
tips.step4.p2: Coming soon: email notifications when new listings open in your preferred cities.
tips.step5.title: 5. After You Apply
tips.step5.p1: After the application window closes, the city or property manager runs a lottery. If your number is drawn, you'll be contacted for income verification and document review. This process can take several weeks to months.
tips.step5.p2: If you aren't selected, stay on any waitlists offered — units turn over, and waitlist applicants are contacted when spots open.
tips.faq.title: Common Questions
tips.faq1.q: Can I use a Section 8 voucher for a BMR unit?
tips.faq1.a: In most cases, yes. Many BMR properties accept Housing Choice Vouchers. The listing details will specify if vouchers are accepted.
tips.faq2.q: Do I need to live or work in the city to apply?
tips.faq2.a: It depends on the city. Some give priority to local residents or workers, but most don't restrict applications by geography. Check each listing's eligibility requirements.
tips.faq3.q: How long do BMR restrictions last?
tips.faq3.a: BMR rental restrictions typically last for the life of the property. For ownership units, deed restrictions usually last 30–55 years and limit resale prices to maintain affordability.
ami.title: AMI Calculator
ami.subtitle: Find out which BMR listings you qualify for based on your household income and size.
ami.whatIsAmi.p1: Area Median Income (AMI) is the midpoint of a region's income distribution, published annually by HUD. In Santa Clara County (which covers most of the Bay Area cities we track), the 2024 AMI for a 4-person household is approximately $168,500.
ami.whatIsAmi.p2: BMR listings specify which AMI bracket they serve. For example, a listing at "80% AMI" means your household income must be at or below 80% of the area median income, adjusted for household size.
ami.incomeLimits.source: Source: HCD State Income Limits. These update annually.
about.title: About Bay Area BMR
about.subtitle: Free, open-source affordable housing data for the Bay Area. Built with public records, maintained for the public good.
about.problem.title: The Problem
about.problem.p1: The Bay Area has thousands of Below Market Rate housing units spread across its cities. But finding them is unreasonably hard. Each city maintains its own system — some post PDFs on municipal websites, others use spreadsheets, a few have no public-facing listing at all. There's no single place to search.
about.problem.p2: BMR units are embedded in luxury apartment complexes with pools, gyms, and modern finishes. But you'd never know that from the way they're listed. The information gap means eligible families miss deadlines, never learn about openings, or don't realize how good these opportunities actually are.
about.solution.title: What We Built
about.solution.p1: Bay Area BMR aggregates every BMR listing across Bay Area cities into one searchable platform. We show you interior photos, amenities, market-rate comparisons, and application deadlines — the same quality of information you'd see on Zillow, for housing you can actually afford.
about.solution.p2: The site is free. The code is open source. The data comes from California Public Records Act (CPRA) requests submitted directly to each city's housing department on a bimonthly cycle.
about.data.title: Where the Data Comes From
about.data.p1: We file formal CPRA requests with Bay Area city housing departments every two months. Cities are legally required to respond within 10 calendar days.
about.data.p2: The data we receive includes property names, addresses, income limits, unit counts, bedroom configurations, and application deadlines. We supplement this with publicly available information from property management company websites, Zillow, Apartments.com, and Google Places to add photos, amenities, and market-rate pricing.
about.data.p3: Every listing shows a "Last Verified" date so you know how current the information is.
about.cities.title: Cities We Cover
about.contact.title: Contact
about.contact.p1: Have a question, correction, or want to help? Reach out:
about.disclaimer.title: Disclaimer
about.disclaimer.p1: Bay Area BMR is an independent civic project. We are not affiliated with, endorsed by, or connected to any city government, housing authority, or property management company. While we strive for accuracy, always verify listing details directly with the property or city housing department before applying. Listing availability and eligibility requirements may change without notice.
value.title: Why Bay Area BMR?
value.subtitle: We built the housing search that should already exist.
value.one.title: Real Data, One Search
value.one.desc: Stop checking multiple city websites. Every BMR listing across Bay Area cities, in one place.
value.two.title: See What You're Getting
value.two.desc: Interior photos, amenities, and market-rate comparisons. BMR units are in luxury buildings — we show you that.
value.three.title: Never Miss a Deadline
value.three.desc: Application windows are short. We track every deadline and will notify you when new listings open in your city.
footer.mission: Free, open-source affordable housing data for the Bay Area. Built with public records. Not affiliated with any government agency.
